currentUse: Private residential building; the authentic church interior reportedly remains, though the cornerstone has been removed
summary: St. Casimir's church building in Pittston still stands and is now used as a residential building; per the Global True Lithuania heritage survey, the authentic interior remains but the cornerstone was removed and nothing outside marks its 99 years (1909-2008) as the hub of Pittston Lithuanians. After the Diocese of Scranton closed the parish in April 2008 and merged it into St. John the Evangelist, the church was sold to a private buyer who planned an arts venue; after the building was vandalized she resold it, and it passed into residential use. Regional press (Pittston Progress) reported the owner as Gina Malsky, who bought the building in 2011 intending a multi-use arts center and put it up for sale after teenage vandals damaged it, though that article is no longer retrievable online. Lithuanian community life persists nearby: Knights of Lithuania Council 143 of Pittston celebrated the Feast of St. Casimir with Mass at St. John the Evangelist Church on March 3, 2024. The successor parish itself has now been dissolved: effective July 1, 2026, St. John the Evangelist, Our Lady of the Eucharist, and St. Joseph Marello parishes were consolidated into the new Communion of Saints Parish, Pittston, under Rev. Joseph P. Elston, as part of the diocese's Vision 2030 restructuring. No sale prices for the church property were found in any published source.

developments.headline: Parish closed and merged into St. John the Evangelist
developments.detail: The Diocese of Scranton closed St. Casimir's; the Times Leader ran 'End Of An Era For Church, Parishioners' on April 7, 2008 (headline and date confirmed in the paper's archive; article body no longer served). Global True Lithuania records the church as the hub of Pittston Lithuanians for 99 years, 1909-2008.

sources.title: End Of An Era For Church, Parishioners
Pennsylvania Northern Coal Region Lithuanian sites

developments.headline: Sold to private buyer Gina Malsky for planned arts center
developments.detail: Per Pittston Progress reporting (surfaced via search; the article now redirects to the paper's homepage and could not be opened), Gina Malsky bought St. Casimir's in 2011 hoping to repurpose it as a multi-use arts venue. Global True Lithuania (opened) corroborates: 'The new owner planned to showcase art here.'

sources.title: Vandalism last straw for St. Casimir's owner
Pennsylvania Northern Coal Region Lithuanian sites

developments.headline: Vandalism prompts owner to resell; building later becomes residential
developments.detail: After the former church was vandalized, the owner sold it again (Global True Lithuania, opened). The building is currently used as a residential building with the authentic interior still in place; even the cornerstone was removed from the facade.

sources.title: Pennsylvania Northern Coal Region Lithuanian sites
Vandalism last straw for St. Casimir's owner

developments.headline: Knights of Lithuania Council 143 still active in Pittston
developments.detail: Council 143 celebrated the Feast of St. Casimir with Mass at St. John the Evangelist Church, Pittston, conferring third- and fourth-degree honors, followed by a luncheon and Amber District meeting in the Monsignor Bendik Center — evidence of continuing organized Lithuanian Catholic community life in the town.

sources.title: Knights of Lithuania Council 143, Pittston Celebrated the feast of Saint Casimir

developments.headline: Successor parish dissolved into Communion of Saints Parish
developments.detail: In clergy appointments announced June 29, 2026, the Diocese of Scranton consolidated Saint John the Evangelist Parish (the parish St. Casimir's was merged into in 2008), Our Lady of the Eucharist Parish, and Saint Joseph Marello Parish into the newly consolidated Communion of Saints Parish, Pittston, effective July 1, 2026, with Rev. Joseph P. Elston, V.F. as pastor. This is part of the diocese's Vision 2030 pastoral planning process. Before consolidation, St. John the Evangelist (35 William Street) held Sunday Mass at 9:30 AM with a Saturday 5:30 PM vigil.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions. The archive record (closed April 6, 2008, merged into St. John the Evangelist, later sold to a private buyer) is fully corroborated: Catholic Directory confirms the closed Lithuanian-language Diocese of Scranton church; press and heritage sources confirm the private sale (buyer identified in regional press as Gina Malsky, 2011). Two refinements: (1) the truelithuania survey dates the church building's era as 1909-2008 — if the archive's 1890 founding date refers to the parish's founding, the standing building is likely a later (c. 1909) structure, worth noting rather than a conflict; (2) the 2008 successor parish, St. John the Evangelist, itself ceased to exist as an independent parish on July 1, 2026, absorbed into the new Communion of Saints Parish, Pittston — the archive's 'merged into St. John the Evangelist' endpoint is now one merger out of date.
gaps: Sale prices were never found in any published source — neither the diocese-to-Malsky sale (2011) nor her subsequent resale; Luzerne County deed records were not accessible via web search. The identity of the current owner/resident is unknown. The exact street address is unverified: Greater Pittston Historical Society material (surfaced in search results as 'Church & Butler Street') could not be opened — greaterpittstonhistory.org returned HTTP 500 and the Facebook post was not fetched — so no address is asserted. The key Pittston Progress vandalism article now redirects to the paper's homepage; its details (Malsky, 2011 purchase, teenage vandals in mid-September, theater-troupe lease, 7,600 sq ft interior) surfaced only in search-result excerpts and could not be independently read, so only the facts corroborated by the opened Global True Lithuania page are asserted in the summary. The precise date of conversion to residential use is unknown. The Global True Lithuania page is undated, though it references events through 2025; its 'residential building' claim could not be double-sourced. Searches run: St. Casimir Pittston closed/sold/demolished/renovated variants, Gina Malsky arts center, Butler Street property records, Diocese of Scranton sale/deed, and successor-parish restructuring. Note: search engines persistently conflate this church with St. Casimir in Pittsburgh (converted to 22nd Street condos) and with Our Lady of Vilna in Eynon (demolished 2024) — both were checked against the opened source page and excluded.
